schools.summary: Bethphage and Bethany joined at the Mount of Olives (Luke 19:29; Matthew 21:1-16).

Bede: having fled kingship (John 6:15), he now accepts it coming to suffer — an everlasting, not temporal, empire; the colt typical, to honour him; Ambrose allegorises the bound colt loosed by Christ.
Henry: he came not incognito but with observation, fearless and cheerful toward his sufferings.
Mark omits the she-ass of Matthew 21:2 (Zechariah 9:9); 'whereon never man sat' (Mark and Luke only); an unyoked beast fit for sacred use (Numbers 19:2).
'The Lord' applied to Jesus by himself (Matthew 21:3); the need grounded in God's claim on all things (Acts 17:25).
Clarke: the words promise to return the colt — Christ borrowed, did not beg, it.
Markan eyewitness detail — the colt at 'the door' outside, at 'the place' where two ways met; the rare word amphodos (only here); exact correspondence to Christ's word.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the woman with the issue of blood, not this verse.

schools.summary: The bystanders, whom Luke 19:33 calls the owners, make a natural protest; the detail (not in Matthew) confirms Christ's foreknowledge.
Gill: the disciples gave the very words Christ appointed.
Clarke: the owners let them go, assured the colt would be safely and speedily restored.
The vivid present 'they bring the colt'; the owners friends of Jesus; the meek King fulfilling Zechariah 9:9, garments cast on the colt as homage.

Mark's peculiar word for leafy boughs ('from the fields'), distinct from Matthew's branches (Matthew 21:8) and John's palms (John 12:13); garments spread as for a king (2 Kings 9:13); the public proclamation of the Messiah.

themes: garments and branches in the way

schools.summary: The cry from Psalm 118:26, the festal welcome of the pilgrim King; the two companies, before and behind, joined in 'Hosanna' (save now).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is a doctrinal passage on the Father and the Son, not directly on the verse.

outliers.note: A doctrinal passage on the Father and the Son, not on the verse.

schools.summary: The blessing of the kingdom as well as the king is peculiar to Mark; the kingdom of 'our father David' (since Messiah is David's son); 'Hosanna in the highest' (Psalm 148:1).

outliers.note: A doctrinal passage on the Father and the Son, not on the verse.
The note here concerns Jairus's daughter, not this verse.

schools.summary: 'Looked round about upon all things' a Markan survey, with reproof (Lightfoot); 'the temple' = the courts; he waited to correct the abuses, God slow to anger (Barnes).
Bede: he went first to the temple, teaching us to turn first to the house of prayer; his poverty found no host but Lazarus; Theophylact: leaving it desolate.
Calvin resolves the order — Mark merely adds what Matthew omitted, that the withering was noticed next day.

debates.question: Do Mark and Matthew differ on the order of the entry, cleansing, and fig tree?

sides.position: No real contradiction — Mark notes what Matthew omits about the next day

schools.summary: 'On the morrow' was Monday morning after the Sunday entry; Matthew has 'early' (Matthew 21:18).
Henry: he went out without breakfast and was hungry, subject to all the sinless infirmities of our nature.
Clarke: 'not the season of gathering figs'; the precocious leafy tree had 'promise without performance' (Robertson); Lightfoot's extraordinary tree; the barren tree as Israel (Isaiah 5:7).
Wesley: the parable's design is to reprove the Jewish Church, leafy in profession but fruitless.

debates.question: Why seek fruit when 'the time of figs was not yet'?

sides.position: 'Not the season of gathering' — fruit, if borne, would not yet be gathered
The precocious leaves promised early fruit, but the tree had none

schools.summary: The verb an optative of wish constituting a curse, spoken in Aramaic; an acted parable, not a fit of disappointment; the disciples listening in amazement.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): as Adam clothed himself with fig-leaves and the ground was cursed (Genesis 3:18), Christ made the fig tree the last sign, that the doom be cancelled.
Bede: what he did in figure (the fig tree) he now shows openly in cleansing the temple; Augustine argues this is a second cleansing, John's (John 2:14) being the first.
Theophylact: a 'den of thieves' of those who sold sacrifices for gain.
Mark's 'began'; the relation to the earlier cleansing in John; the money-changers' trade (Matthew 21:12; Deuteronomy 14:25-26).

debates.question: Is this the same cleansing as John 2:14?

sides.position: No — two cleansings, John's first and this last

schools.summary: The detail peculiar to Mark; the rabbinic rule against making the temple a thoroughfare (Lightfoot); the court used as a short-cut; 'vessel' (keli) the implements of traffic.
Wesley: Christ's strong notions of relative holiness and the regard due to places dedicated to God.

outliers.note: The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: 'For all the nations' (Mark alone) joins Isaiah 56:7 and Jeremiah 7:11; Jesus exercises Messianic authority to smite graft and desecration.
Pseudo-Jerome names the two prophets, Isaiah and Jeremiah, behind the saying.

themes: house of prayer for all nations

schools.summary: The imperfect of a continuous endeavour; Sadducees and Pharisees combine; they feared him because the multitude was astonished at his teaching; the fear peculiar to Mark.
Wesley: afraid to seize him lest it raise a tumult, the people astonished at his majesty and authority.

themes: the rulers seek to destroy him
restrained by fear of the people

schools.summary: 'Whenever evening came' — the imperfect of Christ's nightly custom of leaving the city (Luke 21:37); the time-note peculiar to Mark.
Pseudo-Jerome: the Lord as the sun setting on the Jews to rise on the apostles; Bede and Theophylact: the fig tree dried from the roots = the synagogue smitten for ever.
Henry: all wither who reject Christ; rest in no religion that does not make us fruitful.
They saw it the next morning, 'dried up from the roots' (Mark's detail, perfect participle), Mark separating the two mornings where Matthew 21:20 does not.

themes: the fig tree dried from the roots

schools.summary: 'Peter calling to remembrance' only in Mark, from Peter's story and quick memory; 'cursedst' = devoted to destruction, not implying blame.
'Have faith in God' literally 'the faith of God', a Hebraism for strong faith (objective genitive, Romans 3:22); the lesson from the curse promptly fulfilled.
Wesley: God might destroy a thousand creatures to imprint this lesson on one immortal spirit.
'Shall not doubt' = a divided judgment set against continued faith; the better text 'the thing he saith cometh to pass'; 'this mountain' the Mount of Olives (Matthew 17:20).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is a catechetical passage on eternal life, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the ways of eternal life, not on the verse.

schools.summary: The aorist 'believe that ye received them' sees the fulfilment before it happens; such words imply chiefly spiritual blessings, where faith becomes an objective reality (Matthew 7:7; James 1:5-6).

outliers.note: A passage on the Holy Spirit, not directly on the verse.

schools.summary: Standing not the only posture, God looking on the heart; God's willingness to forgive limited by ours (Matthew 6:14); the Pharisees loved to pray standing (Clarke).
Wesley: forgiving, you shall have whatever you ask, without wrath or doubting.
The verse omitted by Westcott and Hort and wanting in several MSS, but teaching what is plain elsewhere (Matthew 18:35; 6:15) — refusing to forgive forfeits God's pardon.

outliers.note: A doctrinal passage on the angels and the vision of God, not on the verse.

schools.summary: A large committee of the Sanhedrin (chief priests, scribes, elders) in a formal attack on his authority for cleansing and teaching (Matthew 21:23; Luke 20:1).
Theophylact: they came to tempt him; Bede: doubting it was God's power, wishing it thought the devil's, denying he is the Son; the Lord prudently puts a counter-question.
Henry: they claimed power to call prophets to account and arraigned him at the bar.
The question was legitimate in all three Gospels (Matthew 21:23-27); 'these things' = entering the temple as Lord, correcting abuses, teaching; cf. the challenge to Moses (Numbers 16:3).

themes: a fair question in bad faith

schools.summary: Gill: Christ answered, not in the least intimidated by the assembled rulers.
Barnes: he met their demand with 'one question' of his own.
The sharp 'answer me' only in Mark; not a dodge but a home thrust, binding John's authority to Christ's, since they rejected John as they now reject Jesus.

themes: John's baptism from heaven or of men

schools.summary: They see both horns of the dilemma; to say 'from heaven' convicts them of unbelief; they reason by expediency, fearing to turn the laugh on themselves (Robertson, Gill).
The rugged construction (aposiopesis) 'they feared the people'; all counted John a prophet, his martyrdom deepening his power; 'a prophet indeed' a graphic Markan touch.

Their 'we know not' was to escape the trap; their self-imposed ignorance absolved Jesus from a reply, though he does not let them off; the stress on 'Neither do I tell you'; judicial blindness (Isaiah 29:14).
